rationale_for_baseline_tier: An adult found unresponsive (GCS 3) with severe respiratory depression, hypoxia and cyanosis after a mixed opioid/benzodiazepine overdose is in an immediate peri-arrest, life-threatening state. Minutes matter: hypoventilation causes hypoxic brain injury and cardiac arrest. T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier. While waiting, the carer must position the airway, give rescue breaths if needed, and administer any available pre-dosed take-home naloxone. This could not wait for routine or same-day services.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Recognised opioid toxidrome — unresponsive with slow breathing and pinpoint pupils and empty pill packets
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately at first recognition — within seconds, not after further assessment
escalation_edges.rationale: The toxidrome in a peri-arrest patient is an immediate 000 emergency; empirical naloxone and airway support run in parallel with the ambulance call.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is an emergency — call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them he's unconscious and barely breathing after taking pills. Stay on the line with me and them.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He stops breathing, has no normal breathing, or becomes unrousable/blue
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Start rescue breaths / CPR immediately under 000 dispatcher guidance and give naloxone if available
escalation_edges.rationale: Respiratory then cardiac arrest is the lethal endpoint of opioid toxicity; bystander CPR and rescue breathing plus naloxone are the critical bridge to paramedics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he stops breathing or you can't feel breath, lay him flat, tilt his head back, and give rescue breaths — 000 will talk you through CPR. Give the naloxone spray/injection if you have one.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He wakes after naloxone but then becomes drowsy again or his breathing slows (re-sedation as naloxone wears off)
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Give a further naloxone dose and keep the ambulance coming — do NOT cancel it
escalation_edges.rationale: Naloxone's effect is shorter than long-acting opioids; re-sedation and re-arrest are expected, so the ambulance and hospital observation remain mandatory even after apparent recovery.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Even if he wakes up, keep the ambulance coming — the antidote wears off before the drugs do, so he can slip back. If he gets drowsy or his breathing slows again, give another naloxone dose.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: A distressed parent is the sole responder at the scene and must simultaneously call 000, position the airway and give naloxone
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep instructions simple and sequenced: 000 first (on speaker), then recovery position/airway, then naloxone if available, then rescue breaths if not breathing. Stay on the line and reassure.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Mixed overdose with co-ingested benzodiazepine and a chronic opioid use disorder
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Expect incomplete arousal after naloxone (benzodiazepine effect persists) and re-sedation as naloxone wears off — this reinforces, not reduces, the need for 000 and prolonged hospital observation.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them he is unconscious and barely breathing after taking pills. Roll him onto his side in the recovery position and tilt his head back to keep his airway open. If you have a naloxone spray or injection kit, use it now as the instructions say and again after about 3 minutes if there is no change. If he stops breathing, the 000 operator will guide you through rescue breaths and CPR. Do not give him anything to eat or drink and do not try to make him vomit. Unlock the door for the paramedics and stay with him.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 is available 24 hours a day — this is a life-threatening emergency and must not wait for clinic hours.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary; the empty packets should go with the patient to identify the agents. GP and drug-and-alcohol follow-up, and a take-home naloxone kit with family training, should be arranged before discharge.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a comatose, barely-breathing overdose as anything less than an immediate 000 emergency — e.g. advising GP review, watchful waiting or home management.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Continued hypoventilation leads to hypoxic brain injury and fatal respiratory/cardiac arrest within minutes.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Recommending self-transport to an emergency department instead of calling 000 for a peri-arrest patient who needs paramedic airway support and naloxone en route.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: There is no meaningful over-triage risk here — T5 is correct; calling 000 is never the wrong call for this presentation.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The exact drugs and doses taken are unknown, and a co-ingestant could add cardiotoxicity.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Act on the toxidrome — give empirical naloxone for the respiratory depression and escalate to 000 without waiting to identify the agent; let the hospital confirm and screen for co-ingestants.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying naloxone or the 000 call until the precise drug is known, or withholding action because the picture is 'mixed'.
